did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning techniques, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), for skin cancer classification. It implements and compares various optimizers (RMSProp, Nadam, AdaDelta, SGD, Adamax, Adagrad, Adam, and Adam-M) on the HAM10000 dataset of dermatoscopic images to evaluate model accuracy and convergence.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work comparing the performance of various deep learning optimizers (RMSProp, Nadam, AdaDelta, SGD, Adamax, Adagrad, Adam, and Adam-M) on the HAM10000 dataset for skin cancer classification. It includes a methodology section detailing data splitting and cross-validation, results with accuracy metrics and convergence analysis, and a conclusion based on these new findings, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A Comparative Study for Skin Cancer Optimization Based on Deep Learning Techniques
brief_summary: This study investigates the impact of various deep learning optimizers on the accuracy and convergence of skin cancer classification models using the HAM10000 dataset of dermatoscopic images. The authors implemented Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and evaluated eight optimizers: RMSProp, Nadam, AdaDelta, SGD, Adamax, Adagrad, Adam, and Adam-M. The models were trained using 80% of the data for training and 20% for testing, with 10-fold stratified cross-validation to handle class imbalance. Evaluation metrics included classification accuracy, loss validation, and F1-score to assess performance across the seven skin lesion classes. The results indicated that AdaDelta, Adagrad, and SGD exhibited high validation loss and were less suitable for early detection compared to other methods. Ultimately, the customized Adam-M optimizer demonstrated the best performance, offering higher accuracy and faster training times than standard adaptive learning algorithms.

all_task_types.target: Seven distinct skin cancer lesion classes (Melanocytic nevi, Melanoma, Benign keratosis-like lesions, Basal cell carcinoma, Actinic keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma, Vascular lesions, Dermatofibroma)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin cancer using the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (lesion types) using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) optimized with various algorithms (RMSProp, Nadam, AdaDelta, SGD, Adamax, Adagrad, Adam, Adam-M). Evaluation metrics include classification accuracy, loss validation, and F1-score. The loss function used is Sparse Categorical Cross-Entropy, which is standard for multi-class classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, accessible on Kaggle. It contains pre-labeled dermatoscopic images with multiple disease classifications.

use.training: Used for training Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) with 80% of the data allocated for training.
use.validation: Used for validation of loss and accuracy during the training process with various optimizers.
use.testing: Used for testing with 20% of the data allocated for testing.
use.other: Used in 10-fold stratified cross-validation to handle class imbalance and ensure minority classes are present in each fold.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To handle class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset and ensure minority classes are present in each fold during model training and evaluation.
To maintain the class distribution seen in the whole dataset within each fold of the cross-validation, preventing the loss of minority classes.

statistical_methods_used.method: Accuracy, Loss Validation, F1-Score, Precision, Recall
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate and compare the performance and convergence of different deep learning optimizers (RMSProp, Nadam, AdaDelta, SGD, Adamax, Adagrad, Adam, and Adam-M) on skin cancer classification.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It compared optimizer performance using classification accuracy, loss validation, and F1-score via 10-fold stratified cross-validation, but did not perform statistical significance testing between the groups.

HAM10000.notes: All models were trained for 30 epochs with a batch size of 128. The Adam optimizer used a learning rate of 0.0009. The loss function was Sparse Categorical Cross-Entropy. The first class of optimizers (AdaDelta, Adagrad, SGD) encountered local minima and exhibited low accuracy/high loss. The second class (Nadam, Adam, Adam-M, RMSProp, Adamax) showed high accuracy increasing with epochs. Adam-M provided the best overall performance and fastest training time.

architecture_details.description: Deep learning models implemented without feature extraction or preprocessing, dealing directly with dermoscopy images from the HAM10000 dataset.

conclusions: The choice of optimizer significantly impacts the accuracy and convergence speed of deep learning models for skin cancer classification.
Optimizers AdaDelta, Adagrad, and SGD exhibited low accuracy and high validation loss, making them inappropriate for early skin cancer detection.
The Adam optimizer provided the best model performance among the standard adaptive learning algorithms tested.
The customized Adam-M optimizer demonstrated comparatively better results than standard adaptive learning algorithms, offering higher accuracy and faster training times.
The study highlights the crucial role of optimizers in developing accurate and adapted deep learning models for skin cancer lesion classification to assist healthcare professionals.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a single dataset (HAM10000) with no external validation on independent cohorts, limiting generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
The custom 'Adam-M' optimizer is not a standard, widely recognized algorithm, making reproducibility and comparison with existing literature difficult.

The paper does not report specific accuracy percentages or statistical significance tests (p-values), relying instead on qualitative descriptions (e.g., 'high accuracy', 'comparatively better') and visual inspection of convergence plots.
The CNN architecture is described as 'custom' without providing specific layer details, hyperparameters (beyond batch size and epochs), or code availability, hindering reproducibility.
The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance (e.g., 6705 Melanocytic nevi vs. 115 Dermatofibroma), which may bias model performance despite the use of stratified cross-validation.

The study focuses solely on optimizer selection within a custom CNN framework, ignoring the impact of architectural choices, transfer learning, or advanced preprocessing techniques that are common in state-of-the-art skin cancer classification.
The paper is a conference publication with limited scope, lacking the rigorous statistical analysis and comprehensive ablation studies typically expected in high-impact clinical AI research.
